title: The problem with memory systems is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency
summary: An essay on memory systems, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: memory systems once maintenance became the real product
body: My notes on memory systems began with a small irritation in September 2024. The agreement in the room was almost too smooth, and that smoothness made me wary. The smoother the consensus sounds, the more I want to inspect what got removed to smooth it. In this case the omitted piece was the difference between an assistant and a dependency. On the ground, the abstraction dissolves into plain details like upkeep, error burden, sequencing of trust, and who gets to narrate the whole thing as improvement.

Memory systems appealed to me because it sat right on the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering side is easier to describe because it arrives with parts lists, system diagrams, metrics, and demos already attached. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. A tool never arrives alone, it brings a sketch of its ideal user and a guess about the social order it belongs inside. The design tends to confess those assumptions to anyone willing to look closely. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also about the style of dependence the system teaches while everyone is busy calling it useful.

What would not leave me alone was the mismatch between prototype conditions and the world outside them. One virtue of prototype life is that everyone knows the terms are still experimental. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. There is a big difference between testing on willing enthusiasts and asking ordinary people to inherit the result. It is much easier to call something elegant in a prototype than in the institutions that absorb its consequences. The difference between an assistant and a dependency becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

The real shift was not in my taste for the subject but in how little vague language I was willing to tolerate. After writing “What local inference taught me about building futures,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim cannot withstand the texture of a real day, it does not earn much staying power in my mind. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But we should stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and novelty gets marketed as destiny. Memory systems seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we want it.

A final note to myself from September 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. What matters more to me now is who has to carry the system day by day, not who wins the debate around it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: What local inference taught me about building futures
A note on homebound automation when the infrastructure finally became visible
